paragraphs: People around the world have different *morning* routines. These daily habits help them start their day with energy and focus.

In Japan, many people begin their day at 6:00 AM. They drink *water* first, then do light exercise. Japanese workers often eat a traditional *breakfast* with rice, fish, and vegetables. This healthy meal gives them energy for the busy day ahead.

In Saudi Arabia, families often *wake* up early for Fajr prayer. After prayer, some people go back to *sleep* for a few hours. Later, they enjoy Arabic *coffee* with dates or fresh bread. Many Saudi families eat breakfast together before starting work or school.

Scandinavian countries like Sweden and Norway have interesting morning habits. People there take cold *showers* even in winter! They believe cold water helps them feel more awake and healthy. Scientists say this practice can boost the immune system by 29%.

In Brazil, morning routines include fresh fruit juice and strong coffee. Brazilians often *exercise* at the beach or in parks before work. About 60% of people in Rio de Janeiro do some physical activity in the morning. This helps them stay fit and happy.
Every culture has special morning traditions. These routines help people feel ready for their daily activities and connect with their families and communities.

title_ar: How different cultures start their day

paragraphs: Riyadh is a busy city with over 7 million people. Every *morning*, thousands of young Saudis start their day in this modern capital. The city changes quickly, but daily life has its own special rhythm.

Most young people *wake up* early for work or university. They often *eat* a simple breakfast with their *family*. Traditional foods like eggs, cheese, and bread are popular choices. Many people drink tea or Arabic coffee to start their day. After breakfast, they get ready and leave their *home*.

Transportation in Riyadh is changing fast. The new metro system helps people move around the city easily. Many young Saudis also drive cars or use ride-sharing apps. The *work* day usually starts at 8:00 AM. Popular jobs include teaching, business, healthcare, and technology.

Evening time is special for families. People return *home* and spend time together. They often have a big dinner around 8:00 PM. On weekends, many families visit shopping malls, parks, or restaurants. The Boulevard City is a popular place for young people to meet friends and *enjoy* entertainment.

Saudi Arabia is changing, but family and tradition remain important. Young Saudis balance modern life with cultural values. They use technology, study new subjects, and travel the world, but they always remember their roots.

title_ar: How young Saudis live in the modern kingdom

paragraphs: People around the world eat different *food*, but healthy meals are important everywhere. In Japan, people eat fresh *fish* and *rice* every day. In the Mediterranean, families cook with olive oil and fresh *vegetables* like tomatoes and cucumbers. These simple ingredients help people live long, healthy lives.

In Saudi Arabia, traditional meals include dates, yogurt, and grilled meat. *Dates* are very nutritious and give people energy. Many Saudi families also eat *bread* with their meals. This *bread* is often fresh and warm from the oven.

Scientists study food from different countries. They learn that the healthiest people eat lots of vegetables, fruits, and whole grains. For example, people in Greece eat an average of 400 grams of vegetables every day. This is more than people in many other countries.

Cooking at home is becoming popular again. When families *cook* together, they eat healthier food. They also spend less money than buying food from restaurants. Home cooking brings families closer together.

Today, people can learn about food from other countries easily. They watch cooking videos online and try new recipes. This helps people discover *delicious* foods from around the world while staying healthy.

title_ar: Simple ingredients make delicious meals everywhere

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia has many *delicious* traditional desserts. These sweet treats are important parts of Saudi culture and history. Families make them for special celebrations and holidays.

Ma'amoul is a popular *dessert* in Saudi homes. This small cake has dates or nuts inside. People eat ma'amoul during Eid and other happy times. The *recipe* comes from grandmothers to mothers to daughters. Each family has its own special way to make it.

Kunafa is another favorite Saudi sweet. This dessert has cheese and thin pastry. Cooks put *sugar* syrup on top to make it very sweet. In Riyadh and Jeddah, many *restaurants* sell fresh kunafa every day. People often buy it after dinner.

Luqaimat are small round *balls* of fried dough. They are *soft* inside and golden outside. Cooks put honey and sesame seeds on top. These little sweets are perfect with Arabic coffee. Many Saudi families make luqaimat together on weekends.

Saudi desserts use natural ingredients like dates, honey, and nuts. These *ingredients* are healthy and *fresh*. Today, young Saudi women learn these old recipes from their families. They also create new desserts that mix traditional flavors with modern ideas. This keeps Saudi dessert culture alive for future generations.

title_ar: Traditional desserts tell the story of our culture

paragraphs: Every city has its own special character. Some cities are old, and some cities are *new*. Some are *big* with millions of people, and others are small and quiet.

Riyadh is the *capital* of Saudi Arabia. It is home to over 7 million people. The city has many tall *buildings* and wide streets. People live in different *neighborhoods* around the city. Each area has its own shops, restaurants, and parks. The King Abdullah Financial District is very modern with glass towers that shine in the desert sun.

Tokyo, Japan is one of the world's largest cities. Over 37 million people live in the Tokyo area. The city has excellent *transportation*. Trains carry millions of passengers every day. People can travel from one side of the city to the other very quickly. Many Tokyo residents live in small apartments because space is expensive.

Singapore is a city and a country at the same time. It sits on a small island in Southeast Asia. The *government* works hard to keep the city clean and green. There are trees and gardens everywhere. Singapore has strict rules about throwing garbage on the *streets*. The city is famous for its beautiful gardens and clean environment.
Each city faces different challenges. Desert cities need water. Island cities worry about storms. But people everywhere work together to make their cities better places to live.

title_ar: From desert capitals to island nations

paragraphs: Many people think deserts cannot have green *parks* and *beautiful* gardens. But Saudi Arabia shows this is not true. The Kingdom builds amazing green spaces in its cities.

Riyadh has more than 4,000 parks today. King Abdullah Park is one of the largest parks in the Middle East. It has *trees*, flowers, and walking paths. The park also has a *lake* with fountains. Families come here to relax and enjoy nature. Children play in special areas while parents sit in the shade.

Jeddah's waterfront area is another example of green city planning. The Corniche has palm trees along the *beach*. People can walk, exercise, or just watch the Red Sea. The city plants special trees that can live in hot weather. These trees need less water but still look green and healthy.

Creating green spaces in desert cities is not easy. City planners must choose the right plants. They use smart water systems that save water. Some parks use recycled water for the gardens. Solar power helps run the water pumps and *lights* at night.

These green spaces make cities more *comfortable* for people. They clean the air and make temperatures cooler. Parks also give families *places* to spend time together. Saudi cities prove that even in the desert, people can create beautiful, green urban spaces.

title_ar: How Saudi Arabia builds beautiful parks in hot places

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia is home to many amazing *animals*. The country has different environments, from hot deserts to cool mountains. Each place has special animals that live there.

The Arabian Desert covers most of Saudi Arabia. This *hot* and dry place is difficult for animals, but many survive here. The Arabian oryx is a beautiful white antelope. It has long, straight horns and can live without water for many days. Sand cats are *small* desert animals that hunt at night. They have thick fur on their feet to walk on hot sand.

The Red Sea coast has different animals. Here, you can find colorful fish, dolphins, and sea turtles. The water is *warm* all year, so many sea animals *live* in this area. Coral reefs provide homes for hundreds of fish species.

In the Asir Mountains in the south, the weather is cooler. Baboons *climb* trees and eat fruits. These smart animals live in groups called troops. Arabian leopards also live in the mountains, but they are very rare. Only about 200 leopards remain in Saudi Arabia today.

Many Saudi people *love* animals and work to protect them. The Saudi government creates special parks called reserves. These safe places help animals survive and have babies. Learning about local animals helps us understand why we need to *protect* them for future generations.

title_ar: Discovering the wildlife of the Kingdom

paragraphs: The Arabian Desert is one of the hottest places on Earth. During the day, temperatures reach 50°C. Most *desert* animals sleep during these hot hours. They are nocturnal - they are active at night when it is *cool*.

The Arabian oryx is Saudi Arabia's national animal. This beautiful white antelope has long, straight horns. Oryx can live without water for many days. They get water from the *plants* they eat. At night, oryx *travel* many kilometers to find food. Their white fur helps them stay cool.

Many *small* animals also live in the desert. The fennec fox has very big ears. These large ears help it hear *insects* moving in the sand. The ears also help the fox stay cool. Fennec foxes *hunt* at night when insects come out.

Desert animals are very smart. They *hide* under rocks or in holes during the day. This keeps them safe from the hot sun. At night, the desert becomes busy with animals looking for food and water. The Arabian Desert may look empty, but it is full of amazing animals that know how to survive.

title_ar: How animals survive in Saudi Arabia's harsh desert

paragraphs: The Earth has four different *seasons* every year. These seasons bring different types of *weather* to people around the world. Each season has its own special characteristics.

Spring is the season when flowers begin to grow. The *temperature* becomes warmer after the cold winter months. Many trees get new green leaves. In countries like Japan, people celebrate spring when cherry trees bloom with beautiful pink flowers.

Summer is the *hottest* season of the year. The *sun* shines for many hours each day. Temperatures can reach 45°C in some desert areas. People often go to beaches or swimming pools to stay cool. In Saudi Arabia, summer temperatures in Riyadh average 38°C during the day.

Autumn, also called fall, brings *cooler* weather. Leaves on trees change colors from green to red, yellow, and orange. This happens because trees prepare for winter. The temperature drops, and people start wearing warmer clothes.

Winter is the *coldest* season. In many countries, *snow* falls from the sky and covers the ground. However, not all places have snow in winter. Some tropical countries stay warm all year. The shortest day of winter has only about 8 hours of sunlight in many places.
Weather patterns help farmers know when to plant crops. They also help animals prepare for different seasons throughout the year.

title_ar: How weather changes through the year

paragraphs: The Saudi Arabian desert looks *dry* and empty most of the year. But when the *rain* comes, something magical happens. The desert becomes a garden of beautiful *flowers*.

This amazing change happens very quickly. Desert plants wait under the sand for months. Their seeds sleep in the *hot* ground. When water finally falls from the *clouds*, the seeds wake up. In just a few days, colorful flowers cover the desert floor. Red, yellow, and purple blooms make the brown sand look like a rainbow.

The best time to see desert flowers is in *spring*, usually March and April. This is when Saudi Arabia gets its winter rains. The *weather* is perfect - not too hot and not too cold. Many Saudi families drive to the desert during this season. They take photos of the flower carpets and enjoy picnics under the blue sky.

Scientists say desert flowers in Saudi Arabia can *grow* from seed to bloom in just 10-14 days. This is much faster than flowers in other places. The flowers must grow quickly because the rain does not last long. Soon, the hot summer sun returns, and the flowers disappear until next year.
The desert flower season shows us that even the driest places on Earth have hidden beauty. Nature always finds a way to surprise us.

title_ar: How Saudi Arabia's deserts come alive with color

paragraphs: Making friends is important for all people. But how do people make *friends* in different countries? Scientists study this interesting question.

In many countries, people meet friends at work or school. In Japan, work friends often eat dinner together after work. They *talk* about their day and become close. In Brazil, people make friends quickly. They *smile* a lot and are very *friendly* to new people. Brazilian friends often meet at the beach or in parks.

In Saudi Arabia, family and friends are very important. People often meet friends through their *family* or at university. Saudi friends enjoy drinking *coffee* together and sharing meals. They *visit* each other's homes regularly. This makes their friendships very strong.

Social media also changes how people make friends today. A study shows that 68% of young people use apps to meet new friends. They *share* photos and messages online first. Then they meet in person later.

Experts say good friends make people happier and healthier. People with close friends live longer lives. They have less stress and feel more confident. This is true in every country around the world. Friendship is a universal language that everyone understands.

title_ar: How people make friendships in different countries

paragraphs: Every *family* around the world has special traditions. These traditions bring people together and create happy memories. From Saudi Arabia to Japan, families have their own *special* ways to celebrate.

In Saudi Arabia, families often *gather* on Friday afternoons. They *share* a big meal together and talk about their week. *Children* play games while the adults drink tea or coffee. Many families also visit their *grandparents* during the weekend. This time together makes family relationships stronger.

In other countries, families have different traditions. In Mexico, families celebrate the Day of the Dead together. They *remember* family members who are not alive anymore. In China, families gather for Chinese New Year. They eat special food and give red envelopes with money to children.

Food is important in family traditions everywhere. Italian families make pasta together on Sundays. Indian families cook special meals during festivals. In Japan, families eat together every evening. They believe eating together makes the family *happy*.

Research shows that families who spend time together are healthier. Children who eat dinner with their families do better in school. Family traditions help people feel connected to their culture and history. These special moments create memories that last a lifetime.

title_ar: How families celebrate together in different countries

paragraphs: Online shopping is very popular in Saudi Arabia today. Many Saudi women now start their own *businesses* from home. They *sell* clothes, food, and handmade items through social media and websites.

Fatima Al-Rashid lives in Riyadh. She makes beautiful jewelry at home. Every day, she takes photos of her jewelry and posts them online. *Customers* from different cities see her work and want to buy it. "I *earn* good money from my jewelry business," says Fatima. "Last month, I sold 150 pieces."

Many women choose online business because it is convenient. They can work from home and choose their own hours. They do not need to *spend* a lot of money to start. A phone, internet connection, and good products are enough. Some women make traditional Saudi sweets and desserts. Others design modern clothes or make natural beauty products.

Online shopping helps these women become independent. They can *save* money for their families and follow their dreams. The Saudi government supports women in business. There are now special programs to help women learn about online marketing and customer service.

Today, thousands of Saudi women have successful online businesses. They *buy* materials, create products, and build strong relationships with their customers. This new way of shopping and selling is changing Saudi society. It gives women more opportunities and helps the *economy* grow stronger every year.

title_ar: How online shopping creates new jobs

paragraphs: People around the world love to *shop* for things they need and want. Shopping looks different in every country, but everyone needs to *buy* food, clothes, and other items for their families.

In many countries, people go to big shopping centers called malls. These malls have many *stores* inside them. You can find clothing stores, electronics shops, and restaurants all in one place. The Mall of America in the United States has more than 500 stores! In Saudi Arabia, people enjoy shopping at modern malls like Mall of Arabia in Jeddah, which has over 300 shops.

Traditional markets are still very popular around the world. In Morocco, people visit colorful souks to *sell* and buy spices, carpets, and jewelry. Sellers often give *discounts* to customers who ask for better *prices*. In Thailand, floating markets sell fresh fruits and vegetables from boats on the water.

Today, many people prefer online shopping. They can *spend* their *money* from home using their computers or phones. Online shopping grew by 15% in 2023. This new way of shopping is convenient, but many people still enjoy walking through stores and touching products before they buy them.
Whether people shop in malls, traditional markets, or online, shopping is an important part of daily life everywhere.

title_ar: How people buy things in different countries

paragraphs: Your *body* is like a amazing machine that never stops working. Every day, it does thousands of jobs to keep you *healthy* and strong. Scientists study how our bodies work, and they discover new things all the time.

Your *heart* is one of the most important parts of your body. It beats about 100,000 times every day! This strong muscle pumps blood to every part of your body. When you *exercise*, your heart beats faster. This makes your heart stronger, just like lifting weights makes your arm muscles stronger.

Your body needs good *food* to work well. In Saudi Arabia, traditional foods like dates, yogurt, and grilled fish give your body important nutrients. Your *stomach* breaks down the food you eat. Then your blood carries the nutrients to every cell in your body. Drinking water is also very important. Adults need about 8 glasses of water every day.

When you *sleep*, your body repairs itself. Your brain organizes memories from the day. Your muscles grow stronger. Most adults need 7-9 hours of sleep each night. Without enough sleep, people get *sick* more easily. They also find it harder to think clearly and remember things.
Taking care of your body is simple but important. Eat good food, drink water, exercise regularly, and get enough sleep. Your body will thank you by staying strong and healthy for many years.

title_ar: Amazing facts about how your body stays healthy
title_en: Your Body Works Hard Every Day

paragraphs: Your *bones* are amazing. They support your body and protect important organs like your heart and brain. But many people do not think about bone health until they have problems. In Saudi Arabia, doctors say bone health is very important, especially for women.

Your bones need calcium to stay *strong*. Dairy products like milk, cheese, and yogurt are excellent sources of calcium. Green vegetables like spinach and broccoli also help your bones. Many Saudi families eat these foods regularly, which is very good for bone health.

*Exercise* is another key to healthy bones. When you walk, run, or play sports, your bones become stronger. Swimming is also *good* exercise, but walking and running are better for bones because your body works against gravity. Try to exercise for 30 minutes every day.

Vitamin D helps your body use calcium properly. The *sun* gives us vitamin D naturally. In Saudi Arabia, we have lots of sunshine, but many people spend too much time indoors. Try to spend 15 minutes in the morning sun each day. This helps your *skin* make vitamin D.

Your bones are strongest when you are young. After age 30, bones slowly become weaker. But good *food*, regular exercise, and some sunshine help keep bones healthy for your whole *life*. Start taking care of your bones today!

title_ar: How to keep your skeleton healthy every day

paragraphs: People everywhere have different ways to spend their *free time*. Hobbies help us relax and learn new things. They also help us meet new people and have fun.

In many countries, *reading* is a very popular hobby. In Norway, people read more books than anywhere else in the world. The average person reads 20 books every year! Many people also enjoy *cooking* as a hobby. In Italy, families spend hours making pasta and sharing recipes. Young people often learn to cook from their grandparents.

*Music* is another favorite hobby around the world. In South Korea, 73% of teenagers learn to play a musical instrument. The piano and guitar are the most popular choices. Many people also like *dancing*. In Brazil, people of all ages learn traditional dances like samba.

Today, many people enjoy *photography* as a hobby. With smartphones, taking *pictures* is easier than ever. People share millions of photos online every day. They take pictures of food, nature, and family moments.

*Sports* are also very popular hobbies. Swimming is the most popular sport in Australia. In Canada, ice hockey brings families together during winter. These hobbies help people stay healthy and make friends. What hobby do you want to try?

title_ar: How people spend their free time in different countries

paragraphs: Many young Saudi women are learning traditional *crafts* today. These old arts are becoming popular modern hobbies. Women across the Kingdom are using their *free* time to create beautiful handmade items.

Sadu weaving is one popular traditional craft. This art uses sheep and camel wool to make colorful patterns. Women *practice* this skill for many hours each week. They create bags, cushions, and decorative items. The Al-Sadu Society in Kuwait City teaches this ancient art to new students. Many Saudi women travel there to learn.

Another growing hobby is traditional embroidery called *Talli*. This craft uses gold and silver threads to make beautiful designs on fabric. Women *enjoy* making decorative pieces for their homes. They often work together in *groups* to share techniques and ideas. The patterns tell stories about Saudi culture and history.

Pottery is also becoming *popular* again. Young women learn to make traditional coffee pots and decorative bowls. They use clay from different regions of Saudi Arabia. Each area has its own special type of clay. Some pottery workshops in Riyadh and Jeddah teach both traditional and modern techniques.

These traditional crafts help women *relax* and connect with their heritage. They also create income opportunities. Many women sell their handmade items online or at cultural festivals. These ancient arts are finding new life in the modern world.

title_ar: Traditional arts create modern hobbies

paragraphs: Many young people dream of traveling to new countries. Planning your first international trip can feel exciting but also scary. With good preparation, you can have a safe and wonderful experience.

Before you travel, you need important *documents*. Your *passport* is the most important paper you need. It shows your identity and nationality. You also need a *visa* for many countries. Check the requirements for your destination country at least two months before your trip. Keep copies of all documents in your *luggage* and on your phone.

When you *arrive* at the airport, follow the signs to immigration. Show your passport and visa to the officer. They may ask simple questions about your visit. After immigration, collect your bags and go through customs. The *airport* staff can help you if you have questions.

Choosing the right *hotel* is very important for your safety and comfort. Read reviews online before you *book* your room. Look for hotels in safe areas near public transportation. Many hotels offer free internet and breakfast. This can help you save money during your trip.

Saudi Arabia issued over 400,000 passports in 2023, showing that more Saudis want to explore the world. Popular destinations include Dubai, London, and Paris. Remember to check your *ticket* details carefully before you travel. Have a great trip!

title_ar: Simple steps to travel the world safely

paragraphs: Modern travelers use *apps* on their phones to make trips easier. These smart programs help people plan, book, and enjoy their journeys around the world.

Translation apps are very popular with travelers. Google Translate works in over 100 languages and helps people *communicate* in foreign countries. The app can translate text, speech, and even signs through your phone's camera. Many travelers say these apps make them feel more *confident* when they visit new places.

Booking apps save time and money. Apps like Booking.com and Expedia let travelers *compare* hotel prices quickly. They can read reviews from other guests and see photos of rooms before they book. Flight apps like Skyscanner help people find cheap airplane *tickets* to any destination. These apps send alerts when prices go down.

Navigation apps help travelers explore safely. Google Maps works in most countries and shows the best *routes* to any location. It works without internet connection too. The app tells travelers about *traffic* problems and suggests faster ways to travel. In big cities like London or Tokyo, metro apps show the quickest paths on public transportation.

Food apps help hungry travelers find good restaurants. TripAdvisor shows reviews and photos of local food. Many apps can *recommend* restaurants based on your location and preferences. Some apps even show menus in English, making ordering easier for international visitors.

title_ar: Technology makes trips simple and stress-free

paragraphs: Today, smart phones are everywhere. In Saudi Arabia, more than 95% of people own a *mobile* phone. These small *devices* help us do many things every day.

Smart phones *connect* us to family and friends. We can send messages, make video calls, and share photos instantly. Young people in Riyadh and Jeddah use their phones to talk with friends who live far away. They also use *apps* for shopping, learning, and entertainment.

The *technology* in phones is very *advanced* now. Modern phones have powerful cameras, fast internet, and long battery life. They can translate languages, give directions, and even help us pray at the right times. Many Saudi students use their phones to study and take online classes.

Smart phones also help businesses grow. Small shop owners use mobile apps to sell products online. They can reach customers across the Kingdom and even in other countries. This *digital* revolution creates new jobs and opportunities.

However, experts say we must be *careful* with phone use. Too much screen time can hurt our eyes and sleep. It is important to balance technology with real-world activities like spending time with family, exercising, and enjoying nature. Smart phones are powerful tools, but we must use them wisely to improve our lives.

title_ar: How mobile technology connects people around the world

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia is using *smart* technology to build better cities. The Kingdom wants to become a leader in artificial intelligence and robotics by 2030.

In Riyadh, new *robots* work in hospitals and shopping centers. These helpful machines can answer questions, give directions, and carry heavy items. At King Fahd Medical City, robot assistants help doctors and nurses with their daily work. The robots can *move* quickly through the hospital halls and deliver medicine to different rooms.

The NEOM smart city project uses *advanced* technology everywhere. *Cameras* with AI can watch traffic and help reduce accidents. Smart traffic lights change colors based on real traffic conditions. This *system* helps cars move faster and saves time for everyone. NEOM will have over 500,000 AI cameras when it opens.

Saudi students are learning to *program* these new technologies. Universities in Jeddah and Riyadh now offer special courses in robotics and AI. Students learn to create *apps* that can solve real problems in their communities. Many young Saudis want to work in technology companies after graduation.
The government believes that smart technology will improve life for all citizens. By 2030, Saudi Arabia plans to have AI systems in every major city across the Kingdom.

title_ar: How artificial intelligence makes life easier in the Kingdom

paragraphs: The world of work is changing fast. New *jobs* appear every year because of technology and modern life. These careers did not exist twenty years ago, but now they are very popular.

Many young people today work as social media managers. These *workers* help companies talk to customers online. They write posts on Instagram and Twitter. They also answer questions from people who buy products. This job is perfect for people who love using phones and computers every day.

Another new career is app developer. These *employees* create games and programs for mobile phones. In Saudi Arabia, there are more than 34 million smartphone users. App developers make the programs that people use for shopping, learning, and entertainment. They *earn* good money because everyone needs apps.

Food delivery drivers have a busy *job* too. They work for companies like HungerStation and Jahez. These *drivers* take food from restaurants to people's homes. During COVID-19, this work became very important. Many families ordered food online instead of going to restaurants.

Data analysts look at numbers and information all day. Their *work* helps companies make better decisions. For example, they study which products sell well and which do not. Companies *hire* data analysts because information is very valuable in business today.
These new careers show that the future has many exciting possibilities for young people.

title_ar: Technology creates exciting career opportunities

paragraphs: Many young people today have *exciting* jobs that did not exist twenty years ago. These modern careers show how the world of work is changing.

Sarah is a social media *manager* for a travel company in Dubai. She creates posts about beautiful places and talks to customers online. Sarah studied marketing at university, but she learned her social media skills by practicing every day. "I love my job because I help people plan their dream vacations," she says. Sarah *earns* a good salary and works from home three days a week.

In London, Ahmed works as a food *photographer*. He takes beautiful pictures of meals for restaurants and cooking websites. Ahmed started as a hobby photographer, but now famous chefs *hire* him for their cookbooks. "Each day is different," Ahmed explains. "Sometimes I photograph traditional Arabic dishes, sometimes Italian pasta." His *creative* work appears in magazines around the world.

Maria from Spain is a *professional* video game player. She competes in tournaments and has thousands of fans who watch her play online. Gaming companies pay her to use their products. Maria practices eight hours every day and travels to competitions in different countries. "People think gaming is not a real job, but I work very hard," she says.
These *successful* young professionals show that today's job market offers many new opportunities for creative and determined people.

title_ar: Young people share their career stories

paragraphs: Festival clothing around the world is more than just *beautiful* fabric. Every color, pattern, and design tells a story about culture and history. When people wear traditional clothes at festivals, they *celebrate* their identity and share their heritage with others.

In many cultures, specific colors have *special* meanings during festivals. Red often represents good luck and happiness in Chinese New Year celebrations. Women *wear* bright red dresses and decorations to welcome prosperity. In India, people throw colorful powder during Holi festival. The *bright* yellow, pink, and blue colors *represent* joy, love, and new beginnings. Each color has a purpose and meaning.

Festival clothing also shows social connections within communities. Families often *prepare* matching outfits months before big celebrations. Mothers and daughters *spend* hours choosing fabrics and designs together. This preparation time *strengthens* family bonds and passes traditions to younger generations. In Scotland, different clans wear specific tartan patterns during Highland Games. These patterns help people identify their family history and connect with relatives.

Today, festival fashion continues to evolve while keeping traditional elements. Modern designers *combine* ancient patterns with contemporary styles. Young people can honor their culture while expressing personal creativity. Fashion shows now feature traditional festival wear from different countries. This helps people around the world appreciate diverse cultural expressions through clothing.

Festival clothing serves as a bridge between past and present. It helps communities maintain their cultural identity in our globalized world. When someone wears traditional festival dress, they carry forward centuries of stories, beliefs, and artistic traditions. These colorful celebrations remind us that diversity makes our world more interesting and beautiful.

title_ar: How festival clothing creates cultural identity

paragraphs: Every year, millions of people around the world *celebrate* special festivals that bring color, music, and happiness to their communities. These cultural events help people remember their history and share their traditions with family and friends.

One of the most *colorful* festivals in the world is Holi, the Festival of Colors in India. During this spring celebration, people throw bright colored powders at each other and dance in the streets. The festival is very *popular* with young people, and over 200 million people participate each year. The colors represent the arrival of spring and the victory of good over evil.

In Saudi Arabia, the Janadriyah National Festival is a *wonderful* celebration of Saudi culture and heritage. This annual event features traditional music, poetry, handicrafts, and delicious local food. Families *gather* together to enjoy camel races, falconry displays, and folk dances. The festival *attracts* more than two million visitors from around the kingdom and other countries.

Many festivals around the world share similar elements. People wear *special* clothes, prepare *traditional* foods, and perform cultural dances. These celebrations help younger generations learn about their culture and feel *proud* of their heritage. Festivals also bring tourists to different regions, which helps local businesses and communities grow.

Today, social media helps festivals become known worldwide. People share photos and videos of their celebrations, and this *connects* people from different cultures. Whether it's the lantern festivals in China, harvest celebrations in Europe, or cultural festivals in the Middle East, these events remind us that joy and community are universal human experiences.

title_ar: How festivals bring communities together through celebration
title_en: Colors and Joy Around the World

paragraphs: The ocean is home to millions of *amazing* creatures. From tiny fish to giant whales, sea animals live in every part of the water. Scientists believe the ocean contains more than 230,000 different species, but they discover new ones every year.

Many ocean animals are *beautiful* and colorful. Tropical fish *swim* near coral reefs in bright blues, yellows, and reds. These small fish *move* quickly through the warm water to escape from bigger fish. In the Red Sea, near Saudi Arabia, divers can see over 1,200 types of fish. The water there is *clear* and perfect for watching sea life.

Some ocean creatures are *huge* and truly incredible. Blue whales are the largest animals on Earth. They can grow to 30 meters long and weigh 200 tons. These gentle giants *eat* tiny sea animals called krill. A blue whale can consume 4 tons of krill every day during feeding season.

The deep ocean is *dark* and mysterious. Strange fish live there that never see sunlight. Some of these deep-sea creatures *glow* in the dark to find food or communicate with other fish. Scientists use special submarines to *explore* these deep waters. They still find new species regularly.

Ocean animals are *important* for our planet. They help keep the water clean and provide food for millions of people. Many countries work together to protect sea creatures and their homes. When we learn about ocean life, we understand why we must take care of our seas.

title_ar: Discover the incredible animals that live in our seas

paragraphs: Deep in the ocean, far below the *surface*, there is a world of complete darkness. This is the deep sea, where the water pressure is *enormous* and no sunlight can reach. For many years, scientists thought nothing could *survive* in this *harsh* environment. However, they were wrong.

In 2019, researchers used special submarines to *explore* the Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean. This trench is the *deepest* part of our planet's oceans, reaching down 11,000 meters below sea level. The scientists made an *incredible* discovery. They found many strange creatures living in this dark world, including fish with huge eyes, jellyfish that glow in the dark, and worms longer than a school bus.

These deep-sea animals have *adapted* to their difficult home in amazing ways. Some fish produce their own light to find food in the darkness. Others have enormous mouths to catch any food that falls from above. The water temperature in the deep sea is very cold, usually around 2-4 degrees Celsius, but these creatures have special blood that helps them stay alive.

Today, scientists continue to study the deep ocean because they believe it holds many secrets. They estimate that humans have only explored about 5% of the deep sea. Each research trip brings new discoveries. Some researchers think the deep ocean might help us understand how life began on Earth. Others believe we can find new medicines from deep-sea creatures that could help *treat* human diseases.
The deep sea remains one of Earth's last unexplored frontiers. With new technology, scientists hope to uncover more mysteries from this fascinating underwater world.

title_ar: Scientists discover amazing creatures in Earth's deepest waters

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia is making *impressive* progress in space exploration. The Kingdom has big dreams and *solid* plans for the future. In 2023, two Saudi astronauts traveled to the International Space Station. Rayyanah Barnawi became the first Saudi woman in space, and Ali Alqarni joined her on this historic mission.

The Saudi Space Commission *manages* all space activities in the country. They work *carefully* to develop new technologies and train skilled people. The commission has many *exciting* projects planned for the next ten years. They want to build satellites, study Earth from space, and help other countries with space research.

Saudi astronauts do *important* work during their space missions. They conduct scientific experiments and take photos of Earth. Rayyanah Barnawi performed medical research in space that could help people on Earth. She studied how the human body changes in zero gravity. This research is *valuable* for future space travelers.

The Kingdom also *supports* young people who want to study space science. Universities now offer special programs about astronomy and space technology. Students can learn about rocket design, satellite communication, and space medicine. Many young Saudis *dream* of becoming astronauts or space engineers.

Saudi Arabia's space program shows the world that the Kingdom is ready for the future. The country *invests* billions of dollars in space technology every year. By 2030, Saudi Arabia wants to be a leader in space exploration in the Middle East. This ambitious goal matches Vision 2030's plan to diversify the economy and build new industries.

title_ar: The Kingdom's ambitious space program aims high
title_en: Saudi Arabia Reaches for the Stars

paragraphs: Space exploration has always *fascinated* people around the world. For thousands of years, humans looked up at the night sky and wondered what was beyond Earth. Today, we are living in an exciting time when space travel is becoming real.

Modern space exploration began in the 1950s when the first satellites were *launched* into space. Since then, astronauts have traveled to the moon, and *powerful* rockets have carried spacecraft to distant planets. The International Space Station orbits Earth every 90 minutes, and astronauts live there for months at a time. They conduct important *experiments* and take amazing photographs of our planet.

Today, space agencies from many countries work together on space missions. NASA from America, ESA from Europe, and other organizations *collaborate* to explore Mars, Jupiter, and beyond. These missions *require* years of planning and billions of dollars, but they help us understand our universe better. Scientists have discovered that Mars once had water, and they continue to search for signs of life on other planets.

Private companies are also joining space exploration. Companies like SpaceX have made space travel more *affordable* and accessible. They can now *transport* astronauts to the space station and bring them back safely to Earth. Some companies are even planning tourist trips to space for ordinary people.

Space exploration *benefits* everyone on Earth. Satellites help us communicate, predict weather, and navigate with GPS. Medical research in space has led to new treatments for diseases. As we continue to explore space, we *discover* new technologies that improve life on our home planet. The future of space exploration looks very bright, and perhaps one day, humans will live on other worlds.

title_ar: How humans are exploring space today

paragraphs: Twenty years ago, artists needed *expensive* materials like paint, brushes, and canvas to create beautiful artwork. Today, digital art is changing how people make and share creative work around the world.

Digital artists use computers, tablets, and special software to *create* amazing paintings, drawings, and designs. They can *experiment* with thousands of colors without buying paint. They can make mistakes and fix them *instantly* with the click of a button. Sarah Al-Rashid, a digital artist from Riyadh, says, "I can *practice* drawing every day without spending money on new materials. My tablet became my art studio."

The internet helps digital artists share their work with millions of people. Artists can post their creations on social media and art websites. Some artists sell their digital paintings online and make good money. In 2023, the global digital art market was worth over $13 billion. Many young people now choose digital art because it is more *convenient* than traditional painting.

Digital art also creates new job opportunities. Video game companies need digital artists to design characters and backgrounds. Movie studios hire artists to create *special* effects and animation. Advertising agencies want creative people who can make *attractive* designs for websites and social media. These jobs did not exist thirty years ago.

However, some people *worry* that digital art might replace traditional art completely. Art teachers believe both types of art are important. Traditional painting teaches students about color mixing and brush techniques. Digital art teaches them about technology and modern design. The best approach is to *combine* both methods and give students many different ways to express their creativity.

title_ar: How technology creates new possibilities for artists worldwide

paragraphs: Art and music are *universal* languages that people understand everywhere. From the colorful markets of Marrakech to the busy streets of Tokyo, artists *create* beautiful works that speak to our hearts. They use different materials and styles, but their message is always the same: humans need to express themselves.

In Saudi Arabia, traditional art forms are becoming *popular* again among young people. Calligraphy artists *carefully* write Arabic letters in beautiful designs. These artists spend years learning to hold their brushes correctly and make each letter perfect. At the same time, modern Saudi artists *paint* on large canvases in galleries across Riyadh and Jeddah. They mix traditional Arabian colors with contemporary styles to tell new stories about their country.

Music also brings people together in *amazing* ways. Scientists have discovered that listening to music makes our brains release chemicals that make us feel happy. When people *listen* to traditional oud music or modern pop songs, their heart rate often matches the rhythm. This happens to people from every culture and age group. Music festivals around the world prove that melodies can make strangers become friends.

Today, social media helps artists share their work *quickly* with millions of people. A young painter in Abha can post her artwork online and receive comments from art lovers in Paris, New York, and Mumbai within hours. This *instant* connection means that artistic ideas travel faster than ever before. Many artists say this global sharing makes their work more *creative* and inspired.

Whether someone draws, paints, sings, or plays instruments, art remains one of humanity's greatest gifts. It helps us understand each other better and shows us that people everywhere share similar dreams, hopes, and feelings.

title_ar: How creative expression connects cultures around the world

paragraphs: Every year, millions of people *travel* to see the world's most *famous* places. These destinations are special because they show us different cultures, history, and natural beauty. From ancient buildings to modern cities, these places tell amazing stories about our world.

One of the most *popular* tourist destinations is Paris, France. The Eiffel Tower stands 324 meters tall and attracts over 6 million visitors each year. People come from everywhere to see this *incredible* metal structure. Another *wonderful* place is the Great Wall of China. This wall is over 21,000 kilometers long and took hundreds of years to build. Visitors can *walk* along parts of the wall and imagine the workers who built it long ago.

Natural places can be just as *exciting* as man-made ones. Niagara Falls, between Canada and the United States, moves 168,000 cubic meters of water every minute. The sound is so *loud* that people can hear it from far away. In Africa, millions of tourists visit to see wild animals in their natural homes. Safari parks let people watch elephants, lions, and giraffes *safely* from special vehicles.

Some places become famous because they are *unique* in the world. The Dead Sea, between Jordan and Palestine, is so salty that people can float on the water without trying. The salt makes the water very thick and heavy. These special places remind us that our planet has many wonders waiting for us to discover them.

Whether natural or built by humans, famous places connect people from different countries and backgrounds. They help us learn about other cultures and see the beauty that exists everywhere in our world.

title_ar: Discover incredible destinations that capture hearts and minds

paragraphs: Every year, millions of people travel to see *spectacular* light festivals that transform ordinary cities into magical wonderlands. These events combine art, technology, and culture to create unforgettable experiences for visitors from around the world.

The Festival of Lights in Lyon, France, is one of the most *famous* celebrations of its kind. For four nights in December, the entire city becomes a giant outdoor art gallery. Local artists and international designers *create* amazing light shows on buildings, bridges, and monuments. The festival began over 150 years ago when people placed candles in their windows to thank Mary, the mother of Jesus, for protecting their city from disease. Today, more than four million visitors *arrive* each year to witness this *incredible* display.

In Singapore, the annual light festival *transforms* the Gardens by the Bay into a colorful paradise. Giant artificial trees, called Supertrees, *glow* with thousands of LED lights that dance to music. The show runs every evening and is completely free for visitors. These 50-meter tall structures also collect rainwater and generate solar energy during the day, making them both beautiful and environmentally friendly.

Saudi Arabia has joined this global tradition with its own *brilliant* light festivals. The Noor Riyadh festival *attracts* thousands of visitors to the capital city each year. International and local artists use light to tell stories about Saudi culture and history. The festival features over 190 artworks across the city, making Riyadh one of the largest outdoor art galleries in the world.

These festivals prove that light can *connect* people across different cultures and backgrounds. Whether in France, Singapore, or Saudi Arabia, families gather together under the night sky to share moments of wonder and joy. Light festivals remind us that art and beauty can bring communities together in powerful and meaningful ways.

title_ar: How cities around the world celebrate with brilliant displays

paragraphs: Every day, we use hundreds of *inventions* without thinking about them. From the moment we wake up to an alarm clock to the time we turn off the lights at night, inventions help us live better lives. But many of the most important inventions started as *simple* ideas that solved everyday problems.

The wheel is one of humanity's greatest inventions. People *created* the first wheels around 3500 BC in Mesopotamia, which is now modern-day Iraq. At first, people used wheels to make pottery. Later, they *discovered* that wheels could help move heavy things. This *amazing* invention changed transportation forever. Today, we see wheels everywhere - on cars, bicycles, shopping carts, and suitcases.

Another *useful* invention is the refrigerator. Before refrigerators, people had to eat food *quickly* or it would become bad. In hot countries like Saudi Arabia, keeping food fresh was very difficult. The first electric refrigerator appeared in 1911, but it was very expensive. By 1950, most American families owned one. This invention *helped* people store food safely and reduced food waste by 40%.

Some inventions seem so *obvious* now that we cannot imagine life without them. The zipper, invented in 1893, replaced buttons and made getting dressed much faster. The paper clip, created in 1867, became the *perfect* way to hold papers together. These small inventions prove that you don't need to be a famous scientist to create something important.

Today, inventors around the world continue to solve problems and make life easier. Many successful inventors say the same thing: the best inventions start with noticing a problem in daily life and thinking of a better way to do things.

title_ar: How everyday inventions made life better for millions

paragraphs: Many people think modern medicine started in Europe, but this is not *accurate*. The truth is that Arab and Muslim scientists made *incredible* medical inventions between 800 and 1400 CE. These inventions still help doctors *treat* patients today.

Al-Razi, a Persian doctor who lived in Baghdad, was the first person to *identify* the difference between smallpox and measles. Before his work, doctors could not tell these diseases apart. He also invented the first *flexible* surgical tools. Al-Razi wrote more than 200 medical books that European doctors *studied* for centuries.

In Cordoba, Spain, Al-Zahrawi became known as the father of modern surgery. He *created* over 200 surgical instruments, including the first surgical scissors and forceps. His most important invention was surgical thread made from cat intestines. This thread could *dissolve* inside the body after surgery, so patients did not need a second operation to remove it. Today, doctors still use this same idea.

Ibn al-Nafis, working in Damascus, made another *remarkable* discovery. He was the first doctor to correctly describe how blood moves through the heart and lungs. European doctors did not understand this process until 300 years later. His work helped future doctors *develop* better treatments for heart problems.

These Arab scientists worked during the Islamic Golden Age, when Muslim cities like Baghdad, Cairo, and Cordoba had the world's best hospitals and medical schools. They translated Greek medical texts, added their own discoveries, and created the foundation of modern medicine. Without their inventions, healthcare today would be very different.

title_ar: How Muslim scientists revolutionized healthcare 1,000 years ago
title_en: Ancient Arab Inventions That Changed Medicine

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia is quickly becoming home to many *talented* sports stars. The Kingdom now supports athletes in soccer, tennis, boxing, and many other sports. These *champions* represent their country with pride in international competitions around the world.

Sarah Al-Attar made history as one of the first Saudi women to *compete* in the Olympics. She ran in the 800-meter race at the 2012 London Olympics. Her *brave* decision opened doors for many other Saudi women athletes. Today, more than 2,000 Saudi women actively participate in organized sports programs across the Kingdom.

Football remains the most *popular* sport in Saudi Arabia. The national team *successfully* qualified for the 2018 and 2022 World Cups. Captain Salman Al-Faraj became a *hero* to millions of Saudi fans. He *scored* important goals and led his team with great skill. The Saudi Professional League now attracts world-famous players like Cristiano Ronaldo and Karim Benzema.

The government invests heavily in sports development through Vision 2030. New sports academies *train* young athletes from age six to eighteen. These programs focus on both physical fitness and mental strength. Sports scientists work with coaches to help athletes reach their full potential. The Kingdom spent over $2 billion on sports infrastructure in the past five years.

Today's Saudi athletes *inspire* the next generation of sports stars. They show that with hard work and dedication, anyone can achieve their dreams. Whether in individual sports like tennis or team sports like basketball, Saudi Arabia continues to produce world-class athletes who make their nation proud.

title_ar: How the Kingdom is creating champions in global competitions

paragraphs: Sports have always been about pushing limits, but today's female athletes are doing something even more *powerful*. They are breaking down old barriers and showing the world what women can achieve. From tennis courts to soccer fields, these *brave* women are making history every day.

Sarah Al-Attar made headlines when she became the first Saudi woman to *compete* in track and field at the Olympics in 2012. She ran the 800-meter race in London, and her participation was a *significant* moment for Saudi sports. Today, more Saudi women are following her path. They *train* hard every day, working with coaches and using modern equipment to improve their skills.

The numbers tell an amazing story. In 2021, women made up 49% of all Olympic athletes, compared to just 23% in 1988. This *dramatic* increase shows how much has changed. Female athletes now *earn* millions of dollars from sponsorships and prize money. Tennis player Naomi Osaka earned $55 million in 2021, making her the highest-paid female athlete in the world.

These athletes don't just win medals – they *inspire* millions of young girls. When Serena Williams played tennis, she showed *incredible* strength and determination. Her success opened doors for other players from different backgrounds. Similarly, when footballer Megan Rapinoe scored goals for her team, she proved that women's sports could be just as exciting as men's sports.

Today's sports stars understand their *responsibility* to be role models. They visit schools, support charities, and encourage young people to follow their dreams. Through their hard work and dedication, they are creating a better future for the next generation of athletes.

title_ar: How female athletes are changing the game worldwide

paragraphs: The Great Pyramid of Giza in Egypt is one of the most *incredible* structures ever built by humans. This ancient wonder was *constructed* over 4,500 years ago for Pharaoh Khufu, and it remained the world's tallest building for almost 4,000 years. The pyramid stands 146 meters high and contains approximately 2.3 million stone blocks.

Building the pyramids required *thousands* of workers who labored for many years. These skilled builders had to *transport* massive stone blocks from quarries located far away. Each block weighed between 2 to 15 tons. Workers used wooden rollers, ramps, and ropes to move these heavy stones. They worked *carefully* to place each block in exactly the right position.

The construction process was *extremely* complex and required advanced planning. Ancient Egyptian engineers designed the pyramids with mathematical precision. The base of the Great Pyramid covers 13 acres of land, and each side is perfectly aligned with the four directions: north, south, east, and west. Scientists believe it took approximately 20 years to *complete* this magnificent structure.

Today, archaeologists continue to make *exciting* discoveries about pyramid construction. Recent studies using modern technology have revealed hidden chambers inside the Great Pyramid. These findings help us understand how ancient Egyptians achieved such remarkable engineering without modern machines. The pyramids *demonstrate* the incredible skills and knowledge of these ancient civilizations.

Visiting the pyramids remains a *popular* experience for millions of tourists each year. Standing before these ancient monuments, visitors can appreciate the dedication and expertise of the builders who created these lasting symbols of human achievement thousands of years ago.

title_ar: How ancient builders created wonders that still stand today

paragraphs: Deep under the desert sand and jungle leaves, *ancient* cities wait for discovery. These lost places tell amazing stories about people who lived thousands of years ago. Archaeologists are scientists who *carefully* dig up these old cities to learn about past civilizations.

One of the most *famous* ancient cities is Pompeii in Italy. In 79 AD, a volcano *suddenly* covered this Roman city with ash and rock. The ash *protected* the city for almost 2,000 years. Today, visitors can walk through Pompeii's streets and see houses, shops, and even food that people left behind. The city shows us exactly how Romans lived, worked, and ate their meals.

In Cambodia, the *magnificent* temple city of Angkor Wat *attracts* millions of visitors every year. Built in the 12th century, this *enormous* stone city was once the center of the Khmer Empire. The temples cover 400 square kilometers - that's bigger than many modern cities! Kings and thousands of workers spent 30 years building these beautiful temples with *detailed* stone carvings.

Archaeologists *continue* to find new ancient cities around the world. In 2018, scientists discovered a huge Maya city in Guatemala using special airplane technology. This city had 60,000 buildings hidden under thick jungle trees. Each new discovery helps us understand how ancient people built their homes, grew their food, and created their art.

These ancient civilizations teach us that people have always been creative and intelligent. They built incredible cities, created beautiful art, and developed smart ways to solve problems. Their stories remind us that human civilization has a long and fascinating history.

title_ar: Archaeological discoveries reveal how people lived thousands of years ago

paragraphs: Photography is one of the most *popular* hobbies in the world today. From family gatherings to beautiful sunsets, people love to *capture* special moments with their cameras. In Saudi Arabia, photography has become an important way for young people to express themselves and share their culture with others.

Modern cameras make it *easy* to take great photos. Most people use their smartphones to take pictures because they are *convenient* and always available. However, professional photographers often prefer *digital* cameras because they produce higher quality images. These cameras have special *settings* that help photographers control light, color, and focus. The *lens* is the most important part of any camera because it determines how clear and sharp the photo will be.

Saudi photographers are becoming famous around the world for their *creative* work. They take amazing pictures of the country's landscapes, from the red sands of the Empty Quarter to the modern buildings in Riyadh and Jeddah. Many young Saudi women are learning photography and starting their own businesses. They photograph weddings, family events, and fashion shows. Social media platforms like Instagram have over 2 billion users worldwide, and many Saudi photographers use these apps to *display* their work and attract new clients.

Photography is more than just pointing and clicking a camera. Good photographers must *practice* regularly to improve their skills. They learn about composition, lighting, and timing. They also study the work of famous photographers from different countries. With patience and dedication, anyone can learn to take beautiful photos that tell meaningful stories.

title_ar: How cameras help us tell stories and save memories

paragraphs: Professional photographers face many *challenges* when they work in Saudi Arabia's desert regions. The extreme heat, bright sunlight, and moving sand create difficult conditions. However, these same conditions also create some of the world's most *spectacular* desert photographs.

Many photographers *prefer* to work during the golden hours - early morning and late evening. During these times, the sun is *lower* in the sky and creates beautiful, soft light. The famous photographer Ahmed Al-Rashid says, "The desert looks completely different at sunrise. The sand dunes change from gold to orange to red." He has been photographing Saudi deserts for over fifteen years and has learned to *adapt* to the harsh environment.

Desert photography requires special *equipment* and techniques. Photographers must *protect* their cameras from sand and extreme temperatures. They often use special covers and filters. The Empty Quarter, or Rub' al Khali, covers about 650,000 square kilometers and is one of the most photographed desert locations in the Kingdom. Its massive sand dunes can reach heights of 250 meters.

Today, many young Saudi photographers are becoming *famous* for their desert images on social media. They use both traditional cameras and modern smartphones to capture the beauty of their homeland. These photographers often *travel* long distances to find the perfect shot. Their work shows the world that Saudi Arabia's deserts are not empty places, but lands full of natural beauty and constantly changing colors.

The growing popularity of desert photography has also helped tourism in Saudi Arabia. Visitors from around the world now come to see and photograph the same *amazing* landscapes that local photographers have been capturing for years.

title_ar: How photographers capture stunning images in Saudi Arabia's landscapes

paragraphs: Food is one of the most *powerful* ways people share their culture with others. Around the world, every country has special dishes that tell stories about their history, climate, and traditions. When we eat food from different places, we learn about new cultures and make connections with people from far away.

Many popular world cuisines started hundreds of years ago. Italian pasta became famous because wheat grew well in Italy's *sunny* climate. Japanese sushi developed because Japan is surrounded by ocean and has lots of fresh fish. Indian food uses many *spices* because traders brought them from different parts of Asia. These ingredients *shaped* the way people cooked in each country.

Today, international restaurants are *common* in most big cities. In London, there are over 12,000 restaurants serving food from around the world. People can *taste* Thai curry, Mexican tacos, and Lebanese hummus all in the same neighborhood. Food festivals also *celebrate* different cuisines. The largest food festival in the world happens in Singapore every year, where 50 countries *share* their traditional dishes.

Social media has made world cuisines even more popular. Young people *discover* new recipes on Instagram and YouTube every day. They see colorful photos of Korean bibimbap or Moroccan tagine and want to try making them at home. Food bloggers travel to different countries and *describe* local dishes to millions of followers online.

World cuisines teach us that people everywhere love to cook and eat together. Food connects us across languages and borders. When we try a new dish, we open our minds to different ways of living and thinking. This makes our world smaller and more friendly.

title_ar: How traditional dishes connect different cultures

paragraphs: Long ago, spices were more valuable than gold. Merchants traveled thousands of miles to *trade* these precious seasonings. The spice routes connected Asia, the Middle East, and Europe for over 1,500 years. These ancient paths changed how people cook around the world.

The journey began in tropical places like India, Sri Lanka, and Indonesia. Here, farmers *grew* black pepper, cinnamon, cardamom, and nutmeg in the *warm* climate. These spices were *rare* and expensive because they only grew in certain places. Arab traders were the first to bring these treasures to the Middle East and North Africa.

Spice trading was not easy work. Traders faced many dangers on land and sea. They traveled through *dangerous* deserts and sailed across stormy oceans. A single trip could take two years! Despite these challenges, the spice trade was very *profitable*. Some spices cost more than their weight in silver. Venice became one of the richest cities in Europe because of spice trading.

Today, we can *easily* buy spices from any grocery store. We often forget how these simple ingredients *changed* world history. Spices led to the discovery of new lands and created connections between different cultures. They made food more *delicious* and helped preserve meat before refrigerators existed.

The legacy of the spice routes lives on in modern kitchens. When we add Saudi baharat to our rice or Indian curry powder to our chicken, we continue this ancient tradition. Each spice tells a story of adventure, discovery, and the human desire to make food taste better. The next time you *smell* cinnamon or taste black pepper, remember the brave traders who brought these flavors to your table.

title_ar: How spices traveled the world and changed cooking forever

paragraphs: In today's connected world, millions of people are choosing to take breaks from social media and technology. This practice is called a 'digital detox,' and it is becoming *popular* across many countries, including Saudi Arabia.

Sarah, a 25-year-old graphic designer from Riyadh, decided to *delete* all social media apps from her phone for one month. 'I was spending four hours every day scrolling through Instagram and Twitter,' she explains. 'I wanted to *focus* more on my real relationships and hobbies.' During her digital break, Sarah started reading books again and spent more *quality* time with her family. She felt less stressed and more *creative* at work.

Research shows that the average person checks their phone 96 times per day. Many people report feeling *anxious* when they cannot access their social media accounts. Dr. Ahmad Hassan, a psychologist at King Saud University, says that taking regular breaks from technology can *improve* mental health. 'When people disconnect from their devices, they often sleep better and feel more *peaceful*,' he notes.

Digital detox retreats are now *available* in countries like India, Thailand, and the United States. These special vacation places do not allow phones or computers. Guests spend their time doing yoga, hiking, or learning new skills. Some retreats report that 78% of visitors continue to use less social media after they return home.

The goal is not to stop using technology completely. Instead, people want to create a *healthy* balance between their online and offline lives. As Sarah discovered, sometimes the best way to connect with the world is to first disconnect from our screens.

title_ar: How people are finding balance with technology

paragraphs: Social media has changed how people *communicate* around the world. Today, more than 4.8 billion people use social media platforms to stay *connected* with friends and family. From Saudi Arabia to Japan, people *share* photos, videos, and messages every day.

Different countries have *popular* social media apps. In Saudi Arabia, Instagram and Snapchat are very *common* among young people. They use these apps to share pictures of food, travel, and daily life. In China, people prefer WeChat and Weibo. These platforms help them *chat* with friends and follow news. WhatsApp is the most used messaging app in Brazil, India, and many Middle Eastern countries.

Social media *habits* are different around the world too. In the United States, people spend about 2.5 hours on social media each day. In the Philippines, people spend even more time - almost 4 hours daily! Saudi users are very *active* on social media, especially during Ramadan and Eid celebrations when families share special moments.

Businesses also use social media to *reach* customers. Small companies in Riyadh use Instagram to sell traditional clothes and handmade crafts. Coffee shops in Jeddah post pictures of their drinks to attract young customers. This helps local businesses grow and become more successful.

Social media brings people together, but it's important to use it *wisely*. Experts suggest taking breaks from screens and spending time with family and friends in person. The best social media users know how to balance online and offline life.

title_ar: How people connect online in different countries

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia is making big changes to protect the environment. The Kingdom has started many new projects to create a *cleaner* future for its people. These green living projects show how the country cares about nature and wants to *reduce* pollution.

The Saudi Green Initiative is one of the most important environmental programs in the world. This project plans to plant 10 billion trees across the Kingdom. The trees will help *improve* air quality and make cities cooler. Workers have already started planting trees in Riyadh, Jeddah, and other major cities. The government wants to *increase* green spaces by 40% in the next ten years.

Many Saudi families are learning to live in more *sustainable* ways. They are using less water and electricity at home. Some people buy *electric* cars instead of regular cars. These cars don't produce harmful gases. Solar panels are becoming *popular* on house rooftops. The sun in Saudi Arabia is very strong, so solar energy works very well here.

The Red Sea Project is another example of green living. This new tourist destination will use 100% renewable energy. The project will *protect* coral reefs and sea animals. Visitors can enjoy beautiful beaches while the environment stays safe. The project creates jobs for local people too.

Young Saudis are very excited about these environmental changes. They *participate* in tree planting events and beach cleaning activities. Universities teach students about green technology and environmental science. These young people will help Saudi Arabia become one of the most environmentally friendly countries in the world. The future looks very *bright* for green living in the Kingdom.

title_ar: The Kingdom leads the way in environmental protection

paragraphs: Saudi Arabia is making a *dramatic* change in how it produces energy. The country wants to *generate* 50% of its electricity from renewable sources by 2030. This is part of Saudi Vision 2030, which aims to create a more *sustainable* future for the Kingdom.

The NEOM megacity project in the northwest shows Saudi Arabia's *commitment* to green living. NEOM will be completely powered by renewable energy, including solar and wind power. The city will *consume* zero carbon and produce zero waste. Construction workers are already *installing* thousands of solar panels across the desert landscape. These panels can produce enough electricity to power millions of homes.

Solar energy works perfectly in Saudi Arabia because the country has sunshine almost every day of the year. The desert regions receive more than 2,500 hours of sunlight annually. Scientists *estimate* that Saudi Arabia could produce 20 times more energy than it currently uses, just from solar power alone. This *abundant* sunshine makes the Kingdom one of the best places in the world for solar energy projects.

The Red Sea Project is another example of Saudi Arabia's green future. This luxury tourism destination will be completely *carbon-neutral*. Visitors will enjoy beautiful beaches and coral reefs while staying in hotels powered entirely by renewable energy. The project will also *protect* the natural environment and create jobs for thousands of Saudis.

Experts believe that Saudi Arabia's investment in renewable energy will inspire other countries in the region. The Kingdom is proving that oil-rich nations can successfully transition to clean energy while maintaining economic growth.

title_ar: How the Kingdom leads the Middle East in renewable energy
